text: The patient is positioned supine under general anesthesia. The surgeon makes two 5mm incisions on both sides of the lower abdomen.
The surgeon injects a mixture of lidocaine, adrenaline, and Ringer's solution into the subcutaneous fat of the lower abdomen using a blunt tip cannula.
The surgeon uses a standard 3.5 mm liposuction cannula attached to a 50 ml syringe with a lock to manually collect approximately 100 ml of Lipoaz Pirate from the lower abdomen.
Pressure plasters are applied over the patient's incisions to minimize hematoma formation. The patient is then repositioned to a lithotomy position in preparation for the injection phase.

The surgeon expels the aqueous infranatant fluid from the syringe, leaving only the adipose tissue. Using a three-way stopcock and two ten-ml syringes, the adipose tissue is homogenized and softened by repeatedly shifting it back and forth between the syringes, preparing it for injection.
The surgeon uses a curette to remove granulation tissue from the fistula tract through the external opening. A ureteral catheter is then applied and secured by tying it to the seton.

The surgeon debrides and curettes the internal opening of the fistula tract, preparing it for closure. Three pre-taken absorbable sutures are prepared, including those involving the internal sphincter, to be tied at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon makes two small incisions on each side of the external opening of the fistula. Adipose tissue is injected around the internal opening and along the fistula tract. The injection is repeated at various levels, creating a firm swelling around the fistula tract.

The surgeon uses a sharp Coleman Cobra head fat injection cannula to cut the fistula tract transversely, releasing fibrotic scar tissue and allowing adipose tissue to be interposed between the cut ends. The three pre-taken absorbable sutures at the internal opening are then tied.
